title: Shellac

infobox.value: ([/ʃəˈlæk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [resin](/wiki/Resin "Resin") secreted by the female [lac bug](/wiki/Kerria_lacca "Kerria lacca") on trees in the forests of India and Thailand. Chemically, it is mainly composed of [aleuritic acid](/wiki/Aleuritic_acid "Aleuritic acid"), jalaric acid, shellolic acid, and other natural waxes.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is processed and sold as dry flakes and dissolved in [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") to make liquid shellac, which is used as a brush-on colorant, food [glaze](/wiki/Glazing_agent "Glazing agent") and [wood finish](/wiki/Wood_finish "Wood finish"). Shellac functions as a tough natural [primer](/wiki/Primer_(paint) "Primer (paint)"), [sanding](/wiki/Sanding "Sanding") [sealant](/wiki/Sealant "Sealant"), [tannin](/wiki/Tannin "Tannin")-blocker, [odor](/wiki/Odor "Odor")-blocker, [stain](/wiki/Wood_stain "Wood stain"), and [high-gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(material_appearance) "Gloss (material appearance)") [varnish](/wiki/Varnish "Varnish"). Shellac was once used in electrical applications as it is a good [insulator](/wiki/Electrical_insulation "Electrical insulation") and seals out moisture. [Phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") and 78 rpm [gramophone records](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") were made of shellac until it was replaced by [vinyl](/wiki/Polyvinyl_chloride "Polyvinyl chloride").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Resin secreted by the female lac bug

For the rock band, see [Shellac (band)](/wiki/Shellac_(band) "Shellac (band)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Shellac_varities.png/500px-Shellac_varities.png)](/wiki/File:Shellac_varities.png)

Some of the many different colors of shellac

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Shellac_liquid_%28cropped%29.jpeg/250px-Shellac_liquid_%28cropped%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Shellac_liquid_(cropped).jpeg)

Shellac in alcohol

**Shellac** ([/ʃəˈlæk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [resin](/wiki/Resin "Resin") secreted by the female [lac bug](/wiki/Kerria_lacca "Kerria lacca") on trees in the forests of India and Thailand. Chemically, it is mainly composed of [aleuritic acid](/wiki/Aleuritic_acid "Aleuritic acid"), jalaric acid, shellolic acid, and other natural waxes.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is processed and sold as dry flakes and dissolved in [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") to make liquid shellac, which is used as a brush-on colorant, food [glaze](/wiki/Glazing_agent "Glazing agent") and [wood finish](/wiki/Wood_finish "Wood finish"). Shellac functions as a tough natural [primer](/wiki/Primer_(paint) "Primer (paint)"), [sanding](/wiki/Sanding "Sanding") [sealant](/wiki/Sealant "Sealant"), [tannin](/wiki/Tannin "Tannin")-blocker, [odor](/wiki/Odor "Odor")-blocker, [stain](/wiki/Wood_stain "Wood stain"), and [high-gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(material_appearance) "Gloss (material appearance)") [varnish](/wiki/Varnish "Varnish"). Shellac was once used in electrical applications as it is a good [insulator](/wiki/Electrical_insulation "Electrical insulation") and seals out moisture. [Phonograph](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") and 78 rpm [gramophone records](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") were made of shellac until it was replaced by [vinyl](/wiki/Polyvinyl_chloride "Polyvinyl chloride").

From the time shellac replaced oil and wax finishes in the 19th century, it was one of the dominant wood finishes in the western world until it was largely replaced by [nitrocellulose lacquer](/wiki/Nitrocellulose_lacquer "Nitrocellulose lacquer") in the 1920s and 1930s. Besides wood finishing, shellac is used as an ingredient in food, medication and candy as *[confectioner's glaze](/wiki/Resinous_glaze "Resinous glaze")*,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and to preserve harvested [citrus fruit](/wiki/Citrus_fruit "Citrus fruit").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Shellac* comes from *shell* and *[lac](/wiki/Lac_(resin) "Lac (resin)")*, a [partial calque](/wiki/Partial_calque "Partial calque") of [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") *laque en écailles*, 'lac in thin pieces', later *gomme-laque*, 'gum lac'.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Most European languages (except [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") ones and Greek) have borrowed the word for the substance from English or from the [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") equivalent *Schellack*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Production")]

See also: [Lac (resin)](/wiki/Lac_(resin) "Lac (resin)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Kerria-lacca.jpg/250px-Kerria-lacca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kerria-lacca.jpg)

Lac tubes created by *Kerria lacca*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/02-Indian-Insect-Life_-_Harold_Maxwell-Lefroy_-_Kerria-Lacca.jpg/250px-02-Indian-Insect-Life_-_Harold_Maxwell-Lefroy_-_Kerria-Lacca.jpg)](/wiki/File:02-Indian-Insect-Life_-_Harold_Maxwell-Lefroy_-_Kerria-Lacca.jpg)

Drawing of the insect *[Kerria lacca](/wiki/Kerria_lacca "Kerria lacca")* and its shellac tubes, by [Harold Maxwell-Lefroy](/wiki/Harold_Maxwell-Lefroy "Harold Maxwell-Lefroy"), 1909

Shellac is scraped from the [bark](/wiki/Bark_(botany) "Bark (botany)") of the trees where the female lac bug, *[Kerria lacca](/wiki/Kerria_lacca "Kerria lacca")* (order Hemiptera, family [Kerriidae](/wiki/Kerriidae "Kerriidae"), also known as *Laccifer lacca*), secretes it to form a tunnel-like tube as it traverses the branches of the tree. Though these tunnels are sometimes referred to as "[cocoons](/wiki/Cocoon_(silk) "Cocoon (silk)")", they are not cocoons in the entomological sense.[[7]](#cite_note-7) This insect is in the same [superfamily](/wiki/Superfamily_(zoology) "Superfamily (zoology)") as the insect from which [cochineal](/wiki/Cochineal#Dye "Cochineal") is obtained. The insects suck the sap of the tree and excrete "[sticklac](/wiki/Lac_(resin) "Lac (resin)")" almost constantly. The least-coloured shellac is produced when the insects feed on the kusum tree (*[Schleichera](/wiki/Schleichera "Schleichera")*).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The number of lac bugs required to produce 1 kilogram (2.2 lb) of shellac has variously been estimated between 50,000 and 300,000.[[9]](#cite_note-Yacoubou-2010-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Velji-2010-10) The root word [lakh](/wiki/Lakh "Lakh") is a unit in the [Indian numbering system](/wiki/Indian_numbering_system "Indian numbering system") for 100,000 and presumably refers to the huge numbers of insects that swarm on host trees, up to 150 per square inch (23/cm2).[[11]](#cite_note-Berenbaum-1993-11)

The raw shellac, which contains bark shavings and lac bugs removed during scraping, is placed in canvas tubes (much like long socks) and heated over a fire. This causes the shellac to liquefy, and it seeps out of the canvas, leaving the bark and bugs behind. The thick, sticky shellac is then dried into a flat sheet and broken into flakes, or dried into "buttons" (pucks/cakes), then bagged and sold. The end-user then crushes it into a fine powder and mixes it with [ethyl alcohol](/wiki/Ethyl_alcohol "Ethyl alcohol") before use, to dissolve the flakes and make liquid shellac.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Liquid shellac has a limited shelf life (about 1 year), so is sold in dry form for dissolution before use. Liquid shellac sold in hardware stores is often marked with the production (mixing) date, so the consumer can know whether the shellac inside is still good. Some manufacturers (e.g., Zinsser) have ceased labeling shellac with the production date, but the production date may be discernible from the production lot code. Alternatively, old shellac may be tested to see if it is still usable: a few drops on glass should dry to a hard surface in roughly 15 minutes. Shellac that remains tacky for a long time is no longer usable. Storage life depends on peak temperature, so refrigeration extends shelf life.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The thickness (concentration) of shellac is measured by the unit "pound cut", referring to the amount (in pounds) of shellac flakes dissolved in a gallon of denatured alcohol. For example: a 1-lb. cut of shellac is the strength obtained by dissolving one pound of shellac flakes in a gallon of alcohol (equivalent to 120 grams per litre).[[14]](#cite_note-14) Most pre-mixed commercial preparations come at a 3-lb. cut. Multiple thin layers of shellac produce a significantly better end result than a few thick layers. Thick layers of shellac do not adhere to the substrate or to each other well, and thus can peel off with relative ease; in addition, thick shellac will obscure fine details in carved designs in wood and other substrates.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Shellac naturally dries to a high-gloss sheen. For applications where a flatter (less shiny) sheen is desired, products containing amorphous silica, such as "Shellac Flat", may be added to the dissolved shellac.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Shellac naturally contains a small amount of wax (3%–5% by volume), which comes from the lac bug. In some preparations, this wax is removed (the resulting product being called "dewaxed shellac"). This is done for applications where the shellac will be coated with something else (such as paint or varnish), so the topcoat will adhere. Waxy (non-dewaxed) shellac appears milky in liquid form, but dries clear.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Colours and availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colours and availability")]

Shellac comes in many warm colours, ranging from a very light blonde ("platina") to a very dark brown ("garnet"), with many varieties of brown, yellow, orange and red in between. The colour is influenced by the sap of the tree the lac bug is living on and by the time of harvest. Historically, the most commonly sold shellac is called "orange shellac", and was used extensively as a combination stain and protectant for wood panelling and [cabinetry](/wiki/Cabinetry "Cabinetry") in the 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Shellac was once very common anywhere paints or varnishes were sold (such as hardware stores). However, cheaper and more abrasion- and chemical-resistant finishes, such as [polyurethane](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane"), have almost completely replaced it in decorative residential wood finishing such as hardwood floors, wooden [wainscoting](/wiki/Wainscoting "Wainscoting") plank panelling, and kitchen cabinets. These alternative products, however, must be applied over a stain if the user wants the wood to be coloured; clear or blonde shellac may be applied over a stain without affecting the colour of the finished piece, as a protective topcoat. "Wax over shellac" (an application of buffed-on paste wax over several coats of shellac) is often regarded as a beautiful, if fragile, finish for hardwood floors. [Luthiers](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier") still use shellac to *[French polish](/wiki/French_polish "French polish")* fine acoustic stringed instruments, but it has been replaced by synthetic plastic lacquers and varnishes in many workshops, especially high-volume production environments.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Shellac dissolved in alcohol, typically more dilute than as used in French polish, is now commonly sold as "sanding sealer" by several companies. It is used to seal wooden surfaces, often as preparation for a final more durable finish; it reduces the amount of final coating required by reducing its absorption into the wood.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Properties")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Haydn_shellac.jpg/250px-Haydn_shellac.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haydn_shellac.jpg)

A decorative medal made in France in the early 20th century moulded from shellac compound, the same used for [phonograph records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") of the period

Shellac is a natural [bioadhesive](/wiki/Bioadhesive "Bioadhesive") [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") and is chemically similar to synthetic polymers.[[17]](#cite_note-17) It can thus be considered a natural form of [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic").

With a melting point of 75 °C (167 °F), it can be classed as a [thermoplastic](/wiki/Thermoplastic "Thermoplastic") used to bind [wood flour](/wiki/Wood_flour "Wood flour"), the mixture can be [moulded](/wiki/Moulding_(process) "Moulding (process)") with heat and pressure.

Shellac scratches more easily than most lacquers and varnishes, and application is more labour-intensive, which is why it has been replaced by plastic in most areas. Shellac is much softer than [Urushi](/wiki/Urushi "Urushi") lacquer, for instance, which is far superior with regard to both chemical and mechanical resistance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] But damaged shellac can easily be touched up with another coat of shellac (unlike polyurethane, which chemically cures to a solid) because the new coat merges with and bonds to the existing coat(s).

Shellac is [soluble](/wiki/Solubility "Solubility") in [alkaline](/wiki/Alkaline "Alkaline") solutions of [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), [sodium borate](/wiki/Sodium_borate "Sodium borate"), [sodium carbonate](/wiki/Sodium_carbonate "Sodium carbonate"), and [sodium hydroxide](/wiki/Sodium_hydroxide "Sodium hydroxide"), and also in various [organic solvents](/wiki/Organic_solvent "Organic solvent"). When dissolved in alcohol (typically [denatured ethanol](/wiki/Denatured_alcohol "Denatured alcohol")) for application, shellac yields a coating of good durability and hardness.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Upon mild [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") shellac gives a complex mix of [aliphatic](/wiki/Aliphatic "Aliphatic") and [alicyclic](/wiki/Alicyclic "Alicyclic") [hydroxy acids](/wiki/Hydroxy_acids "Hydroxy acids") and their polymers that varies in exact composition depending upon the source of the shellac and the season of collection. The major component of the aliphatic component is [aleuritic acid](/wiki/Aleuritic_acid "Aleuritic acid"), whereas the main alicyclic component is [shellolic acid](/w/index.php?title=Shellolic_acid&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shellolic acid (page does not exist)").[[19]](#cite_note-Merck_Index-19)

Shellac is UV-resistant, and does not darken as it ages (though the wood under it may do so, as in the case of pine).[[20]](#cite_note-Naturalhandyman-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Laksha_-_%E0%B6%BD%E0%B7%8F%E0%B6%9A%E0%B7%8A%E0%B7%82%E0%B7%8F_-_Lacquerware.jpg/250px-Laksha_-_%E0%B6%BD%E0%B7%8F%E0%B6%9A%E0%B7%8A%E0%B7%82%E0%B7%8F_-_Lacquerware.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laksha_-_%E0%B6%BD%E0%B7%8F%E0%B6%9A%E0%B7%8A%E0%B7%82%E0%B7%8F_-_Lacquerware.jpg)

*Laksha* is a traditional form of lacquerware from Sri Lanka which is made from shellac derived from [Lac](/wiki/Lac_(resin) "Lac (resin)") resin.

The earliest written evidence of shellac goes back 3,000 years, but shellac is known to have been used earlier.[[20]](#cite_note-Naturalhandyman-20) According to the ancient Indian epic poem, the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*, an entire palace was coated with dried shellac.[[20]](#cite_note-Naturalhandyman-20)

Shellac was uncommonly used as a dyestuff for as long as there was a trade with the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies"). According to Merrifield,[[21]](#cite_note-Merrifield-1849-21) shellac was first used as a binding agent in artist's pigments in Spain in the year 1220.

The use of overall paint or varnish decoration on large pieces of furniture was first popularised in Venice (then later throughout Italy). There are a number of 13th-century references to painted or varnished [cassone](/wiki/Cassone "Cassone"), often [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") cassone that were made deliberately impressive as part of dynastic marriages. The definition of varnish is not always clear, but it seems to have been a [spirit varnish](/wiki/Varnish#Shellac "Varnish") based on [gum benjamin](/wiki/Benzoin_resin "Benzoin resin") or [mastic](/wiki/Mastic_(plant_resin) "Mastic (plant resin)"), both traded around the Mediterranean. At some time, shellac began to be used as well. An article from the *Journal of the American Institute of Conservation* describes using [infrared spectroscopy](/wiki/Infrared_spectroscopy "Infrared spectroscopy") to identify shellac coating on a 16th-century cassone.[[22]](#cite_note-Derrick-1992-22) This is also the period in history where "varnisher" was identified as a distinct trade, separate from both carpenter and artist.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Another use for shellac is [sealing wax](/wiki/Sealing_wax "Sealing wax").[[23]](#cite_note-Woods-1994-23) The widespread use of shellac seals in Europe dates back to the [17th century](/wiki/17th_century "17th century"), thanks to the increasing trade with India.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Uses")]

### Historical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical")]

In the early- and mid-twentieth century, orange shellac was used as a one-product finish (combination stain and varnish-like topcoat) on decorative wood panelling used on walls and ceilings in homes, particularly in the US. In the [American South](/wiki/American_South "American South"), use of knotty [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") plank [panelling](/wiki/Panelling "Panelling") covered with orange shellac was once as common in new construction as [drywall](/wiki/Drywall "Drywall") is today. It was also often used on kitchen cabinets and hardwood floors, prior to the advent of [polyurethane](/wiki/Polyurethane "Polyurethane").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Until the advent of [vinyl](/wiki/Polyvinyl_chloride "Polyvinyl chloride"), most [gramophone records](/wiki/Gramophone_records "Gramophone records") were pressed from shellac compounds.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) From 1921 to 1928, 18,000 tons of shellac were used to create 260 million records for Europe.[[11]](#cite_note-Berenbaum-1993-11) In the 1930s, it was estimated that half of all shellac was used for [gramophone records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record").[[27]](#cite_note-The_Mall-1937-27) Use of shellac for records was common until the 1950s and continued into the 1970s in some non-Western countries, as well as for some children's records.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Until recent advances in technology, shellac was the only glue used in the making of [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet") dancers' [pointe shoes](/wiki/Pointe_shoe "Pointe shoe"), to stiffen the box (toe area) to support the dancer en pointe. Many manufacturers of pointe shoes still use the traditional techniques, and many dancers use shellac to revive a softening pair of shoes.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Shellac was historically used as a protective coating on paintings.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sheets of [Braille](/wiki/Braille "Braille") were coated with shellac to help protect them from [wear](/wiki/Wear "Wear") due to being read by hand.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Shellac was used from the mid-nineteenth century to produce small moulded goods such as [picture frames](/wiki/Picture_frame "Picture frame"), [boxes](/wiki/Box "Box"), toilet articles, [jewelry](/wiki/Jewelry "Jewelry"), [inkwells](/wiki/Inkwell "Inkwell") and even [dentures](/wiki/Dentures "Dentures"). Advances in [plastics](/wiki/Plastics "Plastics") have rendered shellac obsolete as a moulding compound.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Shellac (both orange and white varieties) was used both in the field and laboratory to glue and stabilise dinosaur bones until about the mid-1960s. While effective at the time, the long-term negative effects of shellac (being organic in nature) on dinosaur bones and other fossils is debated, and shellac is very rarely used by professional conservators and fossil preparators today.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Shellac was used for fixing [inductor](/wiki/Inductor "Inductor"), [motor](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor"), [generator](/wiki/Electrical_generator "Electrical generator") and [transformer](/wiki/Transformer "Transformer") windings. It was applied directly to single-layer windings in an alcohol solution. For multi-layer windings, the whole coil was submerged in shellac solution, then drained and placed in a warm location to allow the alcohol to evaporate. The shellac locked the wire turns in place, provided extra insulation, prevented movement and vibration and reduced buzz and hum. In motors and generators it also helps transfer force generated by magnetic attraction and repulsion from the windings to the rotor or [armature](/wiki/Armature_(electrical_engineering) "Armature (electrical engineering)"). In more recent times, shellac has been replaced in these applications by synthetic resins such as [polyester resin](/wiki/Polyester_resin "Polyester resin"). Some applications use shellac mixed with other natural or synthetic resins, such as [pine resin](/wiki/Pine_resin "Pine resin") or phenol-[formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde") resin, of which [Bakelite](/wiki/Bakelite "Bakelite") is the best known, for electrical use. Mixed with other resins, [barium sulfate](/wiki/Barium_sulfate "Barium sulfate"), [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate"), [zinc sulfide](/wiki/Zinc_sulfide "Zinc sulfide"), [aluminium oxide](/wiki/Aluminium_oxide "Aluminium oxide") and/or cuprous carbonate ([malachite](/wiki/Malachite "Malachite")), shellac forms a component of heat-cured capping cement used to fasten the caps or bases to the bulbs of electric lamps.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Current uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Current uses")]

It is the central element of the traditional "[French polish](/wiki/French_polish "French polish")" method of [finishing](/wiki/Wood_finishing "Wood finishing") furniture, fine [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument"), and [pianos](/wiki/Piano "Piano").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Shellac, being edible, is used as a [glazing agent](/wiki/Glazing_agent "Glazing agent") on pills (see [excipient](/wiki/Excipient "Excipient")) and sweets, in the form of [pharmaceutical glaze](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_glaze "Pharmaceutical glaze") (or, "confectioner's glaze"). Because of its acidic properties (resisting stomach acids), shellac-coated pills may be used for a timed [enteric](/wiki/Enteric_coating "Enteric coating") or colonic release.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Shellac is used as a 'wax' coating on citrus fruit to prolong its shelf/storage life. It is also used to replace the natural wax of the [apple](/wiki/Apple "Apple"), which is removed during the cleaning process.[[35]](#cite_note-35) When used for this purpose, it has the [food additive](/wiki/Food_additive "Food additive") [E number](/wiki/E_number "E number") E904.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Shellac is an odour and stain blocker and so is often used as the base of "all-purpose" primers. Although its durability against abrasives and many common solvents is not very good, shellac provides an excellent barrier against water vapour penetration. Shellac-based primers are an effective sealant to control odours associated with fire damage.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Shellac has traditionally been used as a dye for cotton and, especially, silk cloth in Thailand, particularly in the north-eastern region.[[38]](#cite_note-Suanmuang-1999-38) It yields a range of warm colours from pale yellow through to dark orange-reds and dark ochre.[[39]](#cite_note-Punyaprasop-2001-39) Naturally dyed silk cloth, including that using shellac, is widely available in the rural northeast, especially in [Ban Khwao District](/wiki/Ban_Khwao_District "Ban Khwao District"), [Chaiyaphum province](/wiki/Chaiyaphum_province "Chaiyaphum province"). The Thai name for the insect and the substance is "khrang" (Thai: ครั่ง).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Wood finish

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Wood finish")]

Wood finishing is one of the most traditional and still popular uses of shellac mixed with solvents or alcohol. This dissolved shellac liquid, applied to a piece of wood, is an evaporative finish: the alcohol of the shellac mixture evaporates, leaving behind a protective film.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Shellac as wood finish is natural and non-toxic in its pure form. A finish made of shellac is UV-resistant. For water-resistance and durability, it does not keep up with synthetic finishing products.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Because it is compatible with most other finishes, shellac is also used as a barrier or primer coat on [wood](/wiki/Wood "Wood") to prevent the bleeding of [resin](/wiki/Resin "Resin") or [pigments](/wiki/Pigments "Pigments") into the final finish, or to prevent [wood stain](/wiki/Wood_stain "Wood stain") from blotching.[[42]](#cite_note-Shellac-42)

#### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shellac&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Other")]

Shellac is used:

* in the tying of artificial flies for trout and salmon, where the shellac was used to seal all trimmed materials at the head of the fly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* in combination with wax for preserving and imparting a shine to [citrus fruits](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus"), such as [lemons](/wiki/Lemon "Lemon") and [oranges](/wiki/Orange_(fruit) "Orange (fruit)").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* in [dental technology](/wiki/Dentistry "Dentistry"), where it is occasionally used in the production of custom impression trays and temporary denture baseplate production.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* as a binder in [India ink](/wiki/India_ink "India ink").[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* for [bicycles](/wiki/Bicycles "Bicycles"), as a protective and decorative coating for [bicycle handlebar](/wiki/Bicycle_handlebar "Bicycle handlebar") tape,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and as a hard-drying adhesive for [tubular tyres](/wiki/Tubular_tyres "Tubular tyres"), particularly for [track racing](/wiki/Track_cycling "Track cycling").[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* for re-attaching ink sacs when restoring vintage [fountain pens](/wiki/Fountain_pen "Fountain pen"), the orange variety preferably.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* applied as a coating with either a standard or modified Huon-Stuehrer nozzle, can be economically micro-sprayed onto various smooth candies, such as chocolate coated peanuts. Irregularities on the surface of the product being sprayed may result in the formation of unsightly aggregates ("lac-aggs") which precludes the use of this technique on foods such as walnuts or raisins.
* for fixing pads to the key-cups of [woodwind](/wiki/Woodwind "Woodwind") instruments.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* for [luthierie](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier") applications, to bind wood fibres down and prevent tear out on the soft [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce") soundboards.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* to stiffen and impart water-resistance to felt hats, for wood finishing[[49]](#cite_note-49) and as a constituent of *gossamer* (or *goss* for short), a cheesecloth fabric coated in shellac and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") solution used in the shell of traditional silk [top](/wiki/Top_hats "Top hats") and riding hats.
* for mounting insects, in the form of a gel adhesive mixture composed of 75% ethyl alcohol.[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* as a binder in the fabrication of [abrasive wheels](/wiki/Abrasive_wheel "Abrasive wheel"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) imparting flexibility and smoothness not found in vitrified (ceramic bond) wheels. 'Elastic' bonded wheels typically contain [plaster of paris](/wiki/Plaster_of_paris "Plaster of paris"), yielding a stronger bond when mixed with shellac; the mixture of dry plaster powder, abrasive (e.g. [corundum](/wiki/Corundum "Corundum")/[aluminium oxide](/wiki/Aluminium_oxide "Aluminium oxide") Al2O3), and shellac are heated and the mixture pressed in a mould.
* in [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks") pyrotechnic compositions as a low-temperature fuel, where it allows the creation of pure 'greens' and 'blues'- colours difficult to achieve with other fuel mixes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* in [jewellery](/wiki/Jewellery "Jewellery"); shellac is often applied to the top of a 'shellac stick' in order to hold small, complex, objects. By melting the shellac, the jeweller can press the object (such as a stone setting mount) into it. The shellac, once cool, can firmly hold the object, allowing it to be manipulated with tools.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* in [watchmaking](/wiki/Watchmaking "Watchmaking"), due to its low melting temperature (about 80–100 °C (176–212 °F)), shellac is used in most mechanical movements to adjust and adhere pallet stones to the [pallet fork](/wiki/Pallet_fork "Pallet fork") and secure the roller jewel to the roller table of the [balance wheel](/wiki/Balance_wheel "Balance wheel"). Also for securing small parts to a 'wax chuck' (faceplate) in a watchmakers' lathe.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
* in the early twentieth century, it was used to protect some military [rifle](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") stocks.[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* in [Jelly Belly](/wiki/Jelly_Belly "Jelly Belly") jelly beans, in combination with [beeswax](/wiki/Beeswax "Beeswax") to give them their final buff and polish.[[55]](#cite_note-55)
* in modern traditional [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery"), shellac is one of the [hot-melt glue/resin](/wiki/Hot-melt_adhesive "Hot-melt adhesive") products used to attach [arrowheads](/wiki/Arrowhead "Arrowhead") to wooden or bamboo [arrow](/wiki/Arrow "Arrow") shafts.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)
* in alcohol solution as [sanding sealer](/wiki/Sanding_sealer "Sanding sealer"), widely sold to seal sanded surfaces, typically wooden surfaces before a final coat of a more durable finish. Similar to French polish but more dilute.[[58]](#cite_note-58)
* as a topcoat in [nail polish](/wiki/Nail_polish "Nail polish") (although not all [nail polish](/wiki/Nail_polish "Nail polish") sold as "shellac" contains shellac, and some [nail polish](/wiki/Nail_polish "Nail polish") not labelled in this way does).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* in sculpture, to seal [plaster](/wiki/Plaster "Plaster") and in conjunction with wax or oil-soaps, to act as a barrier during mold-making processes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* as a dilute solution in the sealing of [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord") soundboards, protecting them from dust and buffering humidity changes while maintaining a bare-wood appearance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* as a waterproofing agent for leather (e.g., for the soles of [figure skate](/wiki/Figure_skate "Figure skate") boots).
* as a way for ballet dancers to harden their [pointe shoes](/wiki/Pointe_shoes "Pointe shoes"), making them last longer.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)
